tier_1_regimens.setting: Potentially curative for localized disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Partial hepatectomy is indicated for patients with adequate liver function (generally CTP Class A without portal hypertension), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, and adequate future liver remnant (≥20% without cirrhosis, ≥30-40% with CTP Class A cirrhosis). Liver resection is controversial in patients with limited multifocal disease or major vascular invasion but can be considered in highly selected situations. Preoperative portal vein embolization should be considered for patients with chronic liver disease being considered for major resection. Patients with initially unresectable disease responding to therapy can be considered for surgery after multidisciplinary consultation. Minimally invasive approaches have been proven safe and effective.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Potentially curative for selected early-stage disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Liver transplantation is recommended for patients meeting UNOS criteria (AFP ≤1000 ng/mL, single lesion ≥2 cm and ≤5 cm, or 2-3 lesions ≥1 cm and ≤3 cm, no macrovascular involvement or extrahepatic disease). Extended criteria are available through UNOS. Patients should be referred to a liver transplant center. Bridge therapy as indicated is recommended to decrease tumor progression and dropout rate while on the transplant waiting list. Patients with tumor characteristics marginally outside UNOS criteria may be considered for transplantation at select institutions. Patients who have undergone successful downstaging therapy (tumor currently meeting Milan criteria) can also be considered.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Curative-intent for small tumors; also bridge/downstage therapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ablation (microwave/radiofrequency or percutaneous ethanol injection) is preferred locoregional therapy for small, properly located tumors. In well-selected patients with tumors ≤3 cm, ablation should be considered as definitive curative-intent treatment when performed at experienced centers. Local control diminishes for tumors 3-5 cm and may require combination approaches. Ablation is not recommended for tumors >5 cm. Percutaneous ethanol injection should be limited to tumors <3 cm and depends on institutional experience. Efficacy of thermal ablation is directly related to tumor size.

tier_1_regimens.setting: For unresectable, liver-confined disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Transarterial chemoembolization (conventional TACE or TACE with drug-eluting beads [DEB-TACE]) is an arterially directed therapy for unresectable HCC. All arterially directed therapies are relatively contraindicated in patients with bilirubin >3 mg/dL unless segmental treatment can be performed. TACE is not recommended in those with CTP Class C liver function (absolute contraindication). TACE may be safe in highly selected patients with limited portal vein tumor invasion. Systemic therapy may be appropriate following TACE if there is evidence of residual/recurrent tumor not amenable to additional local therapies.

tier_1_regimens.setting: For unresectable, liver-confined disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: TARE with Y-90 microspheres is an arterially directed therapy. Delivery of ≥205 Gy to the tumor may be associated with increased overall survival. A dose of >400 Gy to ≤25% of the liver in patients with CTP Class A liver function is recommended. For anatomically limited disease, radiation segmentectomy with Y-90 or ablative dose SBRT should be considered. TARE has an increased risk of radiation-induced liver disease in patients with bilirubin >2 mg/dL. Randomized trials show TARE is not superior to sorafenib for treating advanced HCC but may be appropriate for patients with segmental or lobar portal vein thrombosis.

tier_1_regimens.setting: For unresectable, liver-confined disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: SBRT can be considered as an alternative to ablation/embolization techniques or when these therapies have not been successful or are contraindicated. SBRT (typically 3-5 fractions) can be utilized for patients with 1 to 5 tumors, assuming there is sufficient uninvolved liver and liver radiation tolerance can be respected. Doses ranging between 27.5–60 Gy (in 3-5 fractions; BED10 >72 Gy) is preferred if dose constraints can be met. The majority of data arises from patients with CTP Class A liver disease with growing experience in patients with CTP B7; safety data are limited for CTP B8 or poorer liver function. Safety of liver radiation in CTP Class C cirrhosis has not been established.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 preferred first-line option. Prior to initiation, patients should have adequate endoscopic evaluation and management for esophageal varices within approximately 6 months. Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab. Grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events were reported in 43% of patients receiving the combination vs 46% with sorafenib.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 preferred first-line option. The combination treatment significantly ameliorated OS compared to sorafenib. At 5 years, the HR for OS was 0.76 (95% CI, 0.65-0.89), with an OS rate of 19.6% for the combination vs 9.4% for sorafenib. Serious treatment-related adverse events were reported in 17.5% of patients treated with the combination vs 9.9% with sorafenib.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 preferred first-line option (moved from Other recommended). Counsel regarding risk of immune-mediated toxicity and increased incidence of early death during the first 6 months of treatment based on CheckMate-9DW trial. The trial showed improved overall survival with the combination vs lenvatinib or sorafenib, though there was a higher rate of death in the combination arm during the first 6 months, and 29% of patients required high-dose steroids due to immune-mediated adverse events. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 other recommended first-line option. Durvalumab monotherapy was noninferior to sorafenib. At 5 years, the HR for OS was 0.85 (95% CI, 0.73-1.00), with an OS rate of 14.4% for durvalumab vs 9.4% for sorafenib. Serious treatment-related adverse events occurred in 8.5% of patients treated with durvalumab vs 9.9% with sorafenib.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 other recommended first-line option. The trial was designed to demonstrate non-inferiority or superiority of lenvatinib; the prespecified boundary for non-inferiority was met. Lenvatinib is an inhibitor of VEGFR, fibroblast growth factor receptor, platelet-derived growth factor receptor (PDGFR), and other growth signaling kinases.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 other recommended first-line option. Median OS was significantly longer in the sorafenib arm. The Asia-Pacific phase III trial also showed similar HR (0.68) but lower median OS (6.5 vs 4.2 months). Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor that suppresses tumor cell proliferation and angiogenesis.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 other recommended first-line option. Tislelizumab was noninferior to sorafenib in terms of OS. Median DOR was 36.1 months vs 11.0 months with sorafenib. Treatment-related adverse events grade 3 and higher occurred in 22.2% of tislelizumab patients vs 53.4% with sorafenib.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent-line systemic therapy
tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Other Recommended (first-line) / Useful in certain circumstances (subsequent-line)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B other recommended first-line option. Pembrolizumab is a recommended treatment option for patients regardless of microsatellite instability (MSI) status. It is FDA-approved for MSI-high (MSI-H) tumors in the subsequent-line setting. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab, which has different dosing and administration instructions.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy after progression on sorafenib
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 option for subsequent-line therapy. Cabozantinib is an oral multikinase inhibitor with potent activity against VEGFR1-3 and MET among other targets. The benefits spanned across a range of AFP levels. On-treatment AFP response was higher in the cabozantinib arm and linked to longer OS and PFS.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy after progression on sorafenib

drugs.name: Regorafenib
drugs.schedule: Once daily for the first 21 days of each 28-day cycle

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 option for subsequent-line therapy. Regorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR1-3, PDGFRB, KIT, RET, RAF-1, and other growth signaling kinases. Patients must have progressed on sorafenib and tolerated sorafenib at a dose of 400 mg per day for at least 20 of the prior 28 days.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy after progression on sorafenib
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 useful in certain circumstances option for subsequent-line therapy, indicated only for patients with baseline AFP ≥400 ng/mL. Ramucirumab is a monoclonal antibody against VEGFR2. A pooled analysis of REACH and REACH-2 showed median OS was 8.1 vs 5.0 months (HR 0.69) in patients with AFP ≥400 ng/mL.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances option for subsequent-line therapy for patients who have not been previously treated with a checkpoint inhibitor. The FDA's ODAC voted against maintaining the accelerated approval, but the Panel maintains nivolumab as a treatment option. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab, which has different dosing and administration instructions.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy for recurrent or advanced tumors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances option for patients with MSI-H/dMMR recurrent or advanced tumors that have progressed on or following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options, and who have not been previously treated with a checkpoint inhibitor.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy for advanced tumors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B useful in certain circumstances option for patients with RET gene fusion-positive tumors. Selpercatinib is a selective RET kinase inhibitor.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy for advanced tumors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Useful in certain circumstances options for patients with NTRK1/NTRK2/NTRK3 gene fusion-positive tumors. NTRK fusions are extremely rare in HCC. Studies have demonstrated response rates in the 50% to 75% range in pretreated NTRK fusion-positive tumors.

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for advanced mixed HCC-CCA
tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on limited prospective data and the potential for activity in both HCC and CCA histologies, a regimen of cisplatin/gemcitabine chemotherapy combined with either durvalumab or pembrolizumab immunotherapy is an appropriate choice for first-line therapy. Tumor multigene panel testing (MGPT) should be considered in all patients with advanced stages of cHCC-CCA to identify potential targetable aberrations. A repeat biopsy at tumor progression may be warranted to reassess dominant histology.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy for advanced mixed HCC-CCA
tier_1_regimens.notes: In the absence of a targetable aberration, regimens with demonstrated activity in both HCC and CCA are reasonable options, including nivolumab plus ipilimumab.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy for advanced mixed HCC-CCA
tier_1_regimens.notes: In the absence of a targetable aberration, regimens with demonstrated activity in both HCC and CCA are reasonable options, including regorafenib.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a primary malignancy arising from hepatocytes, the main parenchymal cells of the liver. It is the most common type of primary liver cancer. The tumor typically develops in the setting of chronic liver disease and cirrhosis, though it can also occur in non-cirrhotic livers, particularly in patients with chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection. Histologically, HCC can present as three gross morphologic types: nodular, massive, and diffuse. The nodular type is the most common and is often associated with cirrhosis, characterized by well-circumscribed nodules. The massive type usually occurs in a non-cirrhotic liver, occupying a large area with or without satellite nodules. The less common diffuse type is characterized by diffuse hepatic involvement with numerous small, indistinct tumor nodules throughout the liver. HCC is radiologically characterized by arterial hypervascularity and contrast 'washout' on portal venous phases, reflecting its predominant blood supply from the hepatic artery, unlike the surrounding liver parenchyma which receives dual blood supply. The disease can remain asymptomatic for much of its natural history; symptoms of more advanced disease include jaundice, anorexia, weight loss, malaise, and upper abdominal pain. Paraneoplastic syndromes, though rare, can occur. Diagnosis is established through characteristic imaging features on multiphasic contrast-enhanced CT or MRI in high-risk patients, often without the need for biopsy. The management and prognosis of HCC are heavily influenced by the degree of underlying liver dysfunction, typically assessed using the Child-Turcotte-Pugh (CTP) score, and the extent of tumor burden.

subtypes.name: Steatohepatitic HCC
subtypes.description: A histologic subtype that morphologically resembles steatohepatitis, often associated with underlying metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD).
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Clear cell HCC
subtypes.description: Characterized by hepatocytes with clear cytoplasm due to glycogen or lipid accumulation.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.
subtypes.key_associations: May have a higher prevalence of IDH1 mutations.

subtypes.name: Macrotrabecular HCC
subtypes.description: A variant characterized by thickened trabeculae (>6-10 cells thick) of hepatocytes.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.
subtypes.key_associations: Often associated with aggressive behavior and poor prognosis.

subtypes.name: Scirrhous HCC
subtypes.description: Characterized by a prominent desmoplastic stromal reaction, often with a central scar.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Chromophobe HCC
subtypes.description: A subtype with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent cell membranes, resembling chromophobe renal cell carcinoma.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Fibrolamellar HCC (FLHCC)
subtypes.description: A distinct variant characterized by large oncocytic hepatocytes separated by dense lamellar fibrosis. It is identified by a unique molecular marker, the DNAJB1-PRKACA chimeric transcript, which accurately identifies FLHCC in 79% to 100% of patients. Patients tend to be younger and have a generally better prognosis than those with conventional HCC, though recurrences after resection are common.
subtypes.frequency: Makes up a very small fraction of all HCCs.
subtypes.key_associations: Younger age, absence of hepatitis, cirrhosis, or elevated AFP. Rarely, if ever, associated with chronic viral hepatitis or metabolic liver disease. Can spread to and recur in lymph nodes.

subtypes.name: Neutrophil-rich HCC
subtypes.description: A subtype with a prominent inflammatory infiltrate composed predominantly of neutrophils.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.
subtypes.key_associations: May be associated with a more aggressive course.

subtypes.name: Lymphocyte-rich HCC
subtypes.description: A subtype with a prominent lymphocytic infiltrate, potentially indicating an immune response.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Combined HCC-Cholangiocarcinoma (cHCC-CCA)
subtypes.description: A rare primary liver tumor containing histologic features of both HCC and intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA). It may present as separate foci of both histologies or as a biphenotypic tumor with co-expression of immunohistochemical markers for both lineages. Multigene panel testing suggests a higher prevalence of genomic alterations more commonly associated with HCC (e.g., TP53, TERT promoter mutations) than CCA.
subtypes.frequency: An estimated 1% to 10% of patients with primary liver tumors.
subtypes.key_associations: Diagnosis is challenging via imaging and often requires biopsy. May present with elevated CA 19-9 in addition to AFP. Management often requires a multidisciplinary approach incorporating treatment paradigms for both HCC and iCCA.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Hepatocellular carcinoma is associated with a complex landscape of genomic alterations. The development of HCC is a multistep process often arising from chronic liver inflammation and cirrhosis, leading to genomic instability and clonal expansion. Key oncogenic signaling pathways implicated in HCC include the Wnt/β-catenin, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, RAS/MAPK, MET, and insulin-like growth factor (IGF) pathways. Mutations in the TP53 tumor suppressor gene and in the promoter region of the telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT) gene are common events in HCC pathogenesis. While these molecular alterations are recognized, the source text does not provide specific mutation frequencies for these genes in HCC. The molecular pathogenesis is distinct from other primary liver cancers, such as intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, which harbors different predominant genomic alterations. For example, the fibrolamellar variant of HCC is characterized by a specific recurrent fusion transcript, DNAJB1-PRKACA, which is a defining molecular feature of this subtype. The source text notes that tumor multigene panel testing (MGPT) is not routinely indicated for HCC but should be considered on a case-by-case basis, particularly for patients with advanced disease, atypical histology, or to identify potential targetable alterations in the rare setting of NTRK fusions or RET fusions. The role of immune-related biomarkers such as microsatellite instability (MSI), mismatch repair (MMR) status, tumor mutational burden (TMB), and PD-L1 expression is not established for patient selection in HCC, as immune checkpoint inhibitors have shown clinical benefit without requiring these selections.
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: Hepatocellular carcinoma is a major global health burden. In 2025, it was estimated that 42,240 people in the United States would be diagnosed with liver cancer and intrahepatic bile duct cancer, with approximately 30,090 deaths anticipated. Incidence and mortality rates for liver and intrahepatic bile duct cancers have shown recent stabilization in men but have been increasing in women by approximately 2% annually. The incidence of HCC is highest in individuals of American Indian/Alaska Native descent, and forecast analyses predict that HCC rates will be highest in Black individuals and Hispanic individuals over the next 15 years. Increasing incidence rates are predicted for those born between 1950 and 1959, linked to high rates of hepatitis C viral infection in this cohort. Globally, hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection remains the leading cause of HCC incidence and mortality. Approximately 1.5 million people in the United States are chronically infected with HBV. The annual incidence rate of HCC among patients with hepatitis C virus (HCV) with Child-Turcotte-Pugh (CTP) Class A or B cirrhosis is estimated to be ≥1%. While the majority of HCC cases occur in the setting of cirrhosis from various etiologies, certain populations, particularly those with HBV infection, can develop HCC even in the absence of cirrhosis. The disease typically affects individuals in the 5th to 6th decade of life, with a male predominance. The rising incidence in some regions is also attributed to increasing prevalence of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) and its progressive form, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), which are expected to become leading underlying etiologies for HCC.

risk_factors.detail: Any etiology of cirrhosis is a major risk factor. An estimated 60% to 80% of persons with HCC have underlying cirrhosis, possibly approaching 90% in the United States.

HBV is the leading global cause of HCC. The annual incidence is estimated to be 0.5% in carriers without cirrhosis and 2.5% in those with known cirrhosis. Additional risk factors in HBV carriers without cirrhosis include high HBV DNA levels, HBeAg positivity, family history of HCC, male sex from endemic countries >40 years, female sex from endemic countries >50 years, and being from Africa at an earlier age.
risk_factors.strength: Strong, causal association. HBV vaccination programs have been shown to decrease HCC incidence in children and young adults.

risk_factors.detail: HCV is a major cause of HCC, particularly in the United States and Japan. The annual incidence of HCC among patients with HCV and CTP Class A/B cirrhosis is estimated to be ≥1%. Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) improve sustained virologic response, which may eventually decrease HCC incidence.
Alcohol-related liver disease and MASLD are major contributors to the rise in primary liver disease in the United States. Synergistic interaction with viral hepatitis exists.
risk_factors.strength: Strong risk factor, especially in the context of cirrhosis.

risk_factors.factor: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis (MASH) and Metabolic Syndrome
risk_factors.detail: Metabolic disorders (obesity, diabetes, impaired glucose metabolism, MASLD) are associated with increased HCC risk. MASH is anticipated to replace hepatitis as the most common underlying cause of HCC. Screening for HCC in patients with MASLD and advanced liver fibrosis or cirrhosis is recommended.
risk_factors.strength: Increasingly recognized as a major and growing risk factor.

risk_factors.detail: Includes Wilson's disease, primary sclerosing cholangitis, drug-induced liver disease, chronic right-sided heart failure, autoimmune hepatitis, primary biliary cholangitis, hereditary hemochromatosis, porphyria cutanea tarda, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, and Budd-Chiari syndrome. Cirrhosis from these conditions is generally a prerequisite for HCC development.
risk_factors.strength: Established risk factors for HCC in the context of cirrhosis.

risk_factors.detail: Patients with HDV infection in addition to HBV have a greater risk of developing HCC compared to those with HBV infection alone.
risk_factors.strength: Strong risk factor, particularly in HBV-endemic regions.

risk_factors.detail: HCC has a male predominance. Specific risk factors for HBV carriers without cirrhosis differ by gender and geographic origin.
HCC rates are highest in American Indian/Alaska Native, Black, and Hispanic individuals. Incidence varies by geographic region, correlating with the prevalence of hepatitis B and C.

protective_factors: Hepatitis B vaccination (in children and young adults, reduces future risk)
Successful antiviral therapy for chronic hepatitis B and C (reduces risk of HCC development)

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is frequently diagnosed in the setting of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis, which itself may be asymptomatic. When symptoms occur, they are often nonspecific and related to advanced disease or hepatic decompensation. Classic presentation includes an elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) level discovered during surveillance of a high-risk patient, or the identification of a liver nodule on imaging performed for other reasons. Patients may present with right upper quadrant pain, a palpable mass, weight loss, anorexia, malaise, or jaundice. Physical signs can include hepatomegaly, ascites, or features of chronic liver disease such as spider angiomata or palmar erythema. Paraneoplastic syndromes, although rare, can occur and include hypercholesterolemia, erythrocytosis, hypercalcemia, and hypoglycemia. The diagnosis of combined hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma (cHCC-CCA) is challenging, as it may present similarly to HCC but can also be associated with elevated CA 19-9.

symptoms.name: Right upper quadrant pain
symptoms.detail: Nonspecific pain or discomfort in the area of the liver.

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Yellowing of the skin and sclera, indicating impaired bilirubin metabolism.
symptoms.frequency: Less common at presentation; more typical of advanced disease

signs.name: Ascites
signs.detail: Accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity.
signs.frequency: Seen in patients with advanced cirrhosis or peritoneal involvement

signs.name: Palpable mass
signs.detail: A mass felt in the right upper quadrant.
signs.frequency: Less common; indicates larger tumor size

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic patient detected only through surveillance program.
Incidental finding on imaging done for unrelated reasons.
Presentation with complication of cirrhosis (e.g., variceal bleed, encephalopathy) masking underlying HCC.
HCC developing in a non-cirrhotic liver, which may lead to delayed consideration of the diagnosis.
Fibrolamellar HCC variant, which typically occurs in younger patients without cirrhosis and may be mistaken for focal nodular hyperplasia on imaging.

red_flags: New or rising serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) level in a patient with chronic liver disease.
Solid liver nodule ≥10 mm identified on ultrasound during surveillance.
Imaging features suggestive of malignancy (arterial phase hyperenhancement with washout on venous/delayed phase) in a high-risk patient.
Unexplained deterioration in liver function tests in a patient with known cirrhosis.
Symptoms of advanced disease: significant weight loss, persistent right upper quadrant pain, jaundice.

diagnostic.test: Multiphasic abdominal CT or MRI with contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Primary modality for non-invasive diagnosis and staging. Recommended after a positive screening test or if a lesion is identified incidentally. Essential for assessing tumor characteristics, vascular invasion, and extrahepatic disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Arterial phase hyperenhancement followed by washout (nonperipheral) in the portal venous or delayed phase, enhancing capsule appearance, and threshold growth compared to prior imaging (per LI-RADS criteria).

diagnostic.rationale: Recommended as the primary screening tool every 6 months for high-risk patients. Limited for characterization but useful for initial detection.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of liver nodules. Nodules <10 mm require repeat US in 3-6 months; nodules ≥10 mm warrant further workup with multiphasic imaging.

diagnostic.rationale: Considered a problem-solving tool at centers with expertise for characterization of indeterminate nodules. Not suitable for whole-liver assessment, surveillance, or staging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Can demonstrate arterial hyperenhancement and washout patterns similar to CT/MRI.

diagnostic.rationale: Indicated when imaging does not meet diagnostic criteria for HCC (LI-RADS 5), or in patients not at high risk for HCC (e.g., no cirrhosis, no CHB). Also considered for suspected cHCC-CCA or to confirm metastatic disease. Should be considered before ablation and after multidisciplinary review.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic confirmation of hepatocellular differentiation. Reported parameters include presence of small vessel invasion, poor differentiation, and non-hepatocellular components (e.g., cholangiocytic differentiation).

staging.rationale: Routine evaluation for extrahepatic metastases, as lungs are a common site of spread.
staging.indication: Initial staging workup and for monitoring.

staging.test: Abdomen/Pelvis CT or MRI with contrast
staging.rationale: Essential for local staging to assess number, size, and location of hepatic lesions, vascular invasion, lymph node involvement, and other intra-abdominal metastases.
staging.indication: Initial staging workup and for monitoring.

staging.rationale: Selective use for patients with skeletal symptoms or when bone metastases are suspected.
staging.indication: Clinically indicated for bone pain or suspicion on cross-sectional imaging.

biomarkers.rationale: A serum biomarker used in conjunction with imaging for diagnosis, surveillance, and monitoring treatment response. An elevated AFP in the context of a characteristic liver mass has a high positive predictive value for HCC.
biomarkers.when: Every 6 months during surveillance (alongside US). Also measured at diagnosis and periodically during/after treatment.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, part of standard screening and workup.

biomarkers.test: Des-gamma-carboxy prothrombin (DCP/PIVKA-II) and Lens culinaris agglutinin-reactive AFP (AFP-L3)
biomarkers.rationale: Other serum biomarkers being studied. May be used as complementary assays to AFP for screening and diagnosis.
biomarkers.when: Not universally mandated; may be used in certain clinical scenarios or research settings.

biomarkers.rationale: There is no established indication for routine molecular profiling in HCC. However, it may be considered on a case-by-case basis for patients with atypical histology, cHCC-CCA histology, unusual clinical presentations, or for clinical trial enrollment.
biomarkers.when: Consider in advanced or recurrent disease, especially if biopsy shows atypical or mixed features.

do_not_miss: Multiphasic cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI) for any suspicious liver lesion in a high-risk patient.
Assessment of liver function reserve (CTP score, MELD score) and portal hypertension.
Core needle biopsy when imaging is non-diagnostic or if there is suspicion for alternative diagnoses like cholangiocarcinoma.
avoid: Ultrasound alone for definitive diagnosis or staging of a suspected or known HCC.
FDG-PET/CT for initial diagnosis or staging due to low sensitivity for HCC; it may be considered when there is an equivocal finding on other imaging.
Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) over core needle biopsy in most cases, as core biopsy provides more tissue for architecture assessment and molecular testing.
Routine use of invasive procedures like biopsy when characteristic imaging features are present in a high-risk patient, as the diagnosis can be made non-invasively.

t_stages.description: Solitary tumor >2 cm without vascular invasion.
Solitary tumor >2 cm with vascular invasion, or multiple tumors, none >5 cm.
Multiple tumors, at least one of which is >5 cm.

Single tumor or multiple tumors of any size involving a major branch of the portal vein or hepatic vein, or tumor(s) with direct invasion of adjacent organs other than the gallbladder or with perforation of visceral peritoneum.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very early stage. Solitary tumor ≤2 cm, no vascular invasion, no node metastasis, no distant metastasis.
Early stage. Solitary tumor >2 cm without vascular invasion, no node metastasis, no distant metastasis.
Intermediate stage. Either a solitary tumor >2 cm with vascular invasion, or multiple tumors none >5 cm, no node metastasis, no distant metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (resection, transplant) or locoregional therapy depending on liver function and tumor location.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced stage. Multiple tumors, at least one >5 cm, no node metastasis, no distant metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Locoregional therapy (e.g., TACE), systemic therapy, or clinical trial.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced stage. Tumor(s) involve major vascular structures or directly invade adjacent organs, no node metastasis, no distant metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy or clinical trial. Locoregional therapy may be considered in selected cases.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced stage. Any primary tumor with regional lymph node metastasis, no distant metastasis.
Metastatic stage. Any primary tumor, any nodal status, with distant metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy, best supportive care, or clinical trial.

staging_pearls: The AJCC staging system provides information primarily on pathologic characteristics of resected specimens. For clinical staging, the Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) system is often used, which incorporates tumor burden, liver function (CTP score), and patient performance status to guide treatment decisions.

The presence of vascular invasion is a key prognostic factor and is incorporated into the T category (T1b vs. T2, T2 vs. T3/T4). Major vascular invasion (T4) includes invasion of a main branch of the portal or hepatic vein.
For patients with cirrhosis, the background liver disease stage (e.g., Ishak fibrosis score F0-F6) is a critical component of pathology reporting and influences prognosis and treatment eligibility.
The BCLC system, while not an anatomic staging system, is widely used to stratify patients into treatment groups (Very Early/Early, Intermediate, Advanced, Terminal).

management_principles.overview: The management of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) requires a comprehensive, multimodal approach due to the complexity of treating both the malignancy and the underlying chronic liver disease/cirrhosis. The fundamental philosophy is to integrate curative-intent therapies (surgical resection, liver transplantation, and ablation) whenever feasible, while employing locoregional and systemic therapies for unresectable or metastatic disease. A multidisciplinary team (MDT) is essential for individualized treatment planning [HCC-3, HCC-4, HCC-5, HCC-6]. Treatment decisions are heavily influenced by tumor burden (extent, number, size, location, vascular invasion), liver function (Child-Pugh class, portal hypertension), patient performance status, and comorbidities [HCC-3]. The goals of care range from curative intent for early-stage disease to life-prolonging and palliative intent for advanced disease. Participation in clinical trials is encouraged for all stages [HCC-1, HCC-6].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with potentially resectable or transplantable HCC by tumor burden, and operable by performance status or comorbidity (HCC-4).
by_intent.approach: Liver resection (preferred if criteria met) and/or liver transplantation (preferred if transplant criteria met). For small, properly located tumors, ablation is considered a preferred curative-intent locoregional therapy in well-selected patients during multidisciplinary review. Bridging or downstaging locoregional therapies may be indicated for transplant candidates [HCC-4].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with liver-confined, unresectable HCC who are deemed ineligible for transplant (HCC-5).
by_intent.approach: Locoregional therapies are the primary approach. Options include ablation (preferred), arterially directed therapies (e.g., TACE, TAE, radioembolization), and radiation therapy (RT). Systemic therapy is an option and should be considered for progression on or after locoregional therapy. Clinical trials are also an option [HCC-5].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with extrahepatic/metastatic disease, or liver-confined disease ineligible for resection, transplant, or locoregional therapy (HCC-6).
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy is a mainstay. Clinical trials, best supportive care, and RT (for symptom control or selected sites) are also key options. Locoregional therapy may be reconsidered in select cases after multidisciplinary review [HCC-6].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: All patients with HCC should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary team (MDT). For HCC diagnosis and staging, this includes hepatologists, diagnostic and interventional radiologists, surgeons, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, and pathologists with HCC expertise [HCC-3]. Multidisciplinary review is strongly recommended for treatment planning in all disease stages, including for patients being considered for surgery, locoregional therapy, or systemic therapy [HCC-4, HCC-5, HCC-6, HCC-G]. The MDT approach is associated with improved outcomes, including higher rates of treatment and curative therapies [MS-7].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a critical determinant of treatment eligibility and intent. Patients must be medically fit for major surgery [HCC-F]. Resection and transplantation are indicated for patients with preserved performance status [HCC-4]. Locoregional therapies require evaluation of liver function and performance status. For systemic therapies, most pivotal trials were conducted in patients with ECOG PS 0-1; use in patients with poorer performance status requires caution and individualized decision-making, particularly in the context of underlying liver dysfunction [HCC-I, HCC-E].

nodes.criteria: Potentially resectable or transplantable by tumor burden; and operable by performance status or comorbidity. Undergoes multidisciplinary evaluation to assess liver reserve and comorbidity, and staging workup (HCC-3).

options.label: Resection
options.preference: Preferred if resection criteria are met (CTP Class A, no portal hypertension, suitable tumor location, adequate liver reserve and remnant).
options.recommendation: Liver resection is a potentially curative option. In highly selected patients with CTP Class B, limited resection may be considered [HCC-4, HCC-F].

options.label: Transplantation
options.preference: Preferred if transplant criteria are met (UNOS criteria). Refer to liver transplant center. Bridge therapy as indicated.
options.recommendation: Liver transplantation is a potentially curative option for early HCC with cirrhosis, treating both tumor and underlying liver disease [HCC-4, HCC-F].

options.label: Ablation
options.preference: Preferred locoregional therapy for small, properly located tumors in patients not candidates for surgery.
options.recommendation: Ablation (microwave/radiofrequency or percutaneous ethanol injection) should be considered as definitive curative-intent treatment in well-selected patients with tumors ≤3 cm when performed at experienced centers [HCC-4, HCC-G].

options.label: Arterially Directed Therapies or Radiation Therapy (RT)
options.recommendation: Arterially directed therapies or RT may be considered based on disease characteristics and expertise [HCC-4].

options.label: Systemic Therapy
options.preference: Not first-line for this curable stage but may be considered for specific situations.
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy may be considered if deemed ineligible for transplant and with unresectable disease after locoregional therapy assessment, or for high-risk features warranting adjuvant consideration (though adjuvant data is evolving) [HCC-4].

adjuvant_logic.applies: Yes, for high-risk features after curative-intent therapy.
adjuvant_logic.summary: For patients who undergo curative-intent resection or ablation, the role of adjuvant therapy is evolving. The IMbrave050 trial showed improved recurrence-free survival with atezolizumab + bevacizumab vs. active surveillance, but an updated analysis showed the benefit was not sustained; thus, it is not included as a standard option [MS-41]. Adjuvant antiviral therapy is recommended for patients with viral hepatitis to reduce recurrence [HCC-F, MS-41]. Post-resection, surveillance with cross-sectional imaging and AFP every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months is recommended [HCC-4].

nodes.criteria: Liver-confined, unresectable HCC, deemed ineligible for transplant due to inadequate hepatic reserve, tumor location, or extent of disease.

options.label: Ablation, Arterially Directed Therapies, or RT
options.preference: Locoregional therapies are the primary approach. Choice depends on extent/location, hepatic reserve, and institutional capabilities.
options.recommendation: Ablation is preferred for small tumors. Arterially directed therapies (TACE, TAE, radioembolization) are options for tumors not amenable to ablation. RT (including SBRT) is a treatment option, especially when other therapies have failed or are contraindicated [HCC-5, HCC-G, HCC-H].

options.label: Systemic Therapy
options.preference: An option, particularly if there is progression on or after locoregional therapy.
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy should be considered. Preferred first-line regimens include atezolizumab + bevacizumab, durvalumab + tremelimumab-actl, or ipilimumab + nivolumab [HCC-I, HCC-5].

options.label: Clinical Trial or Best Supportive Care
options.recommendation: Clinical trial enrollment is encouraged. Best supportive care is an option [HCC-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for this pathway; focus is on locoregional or systemic control.

management_pathways.title: Extrahepatic/Metastatic HCC or Ineligible for Resection/Transplant/Locoregional Therapy

nodes.criteria: Extrahepatic/metastatic disease, and/or deemed ineligible for resection, transplant, or locoregional therapy. Consider biopsy for histologic confirmation if not previously done.

options.label: First-Line Systemic Therapy
options.preference: Preferred and Other Recommended options based on clinical trial evidence.
options.recommendation: Preferred options (Category 1): Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab, Durvalumab + Tremelimumab-actl, Ipilimumab + Nivolumab. Other Recommended: Durvalumab, Lenvatinib, Sorafenib, Tislelizumab-jsgr, Pembrolizumab (Category 2B). Selection depends on prior therapy, comorbidities, and access [HCC-I].

options.label: Subsequent-Line Systemic Therapy
options.preference: Options include targeted therapies and re-challenge with certain first-line regimens if not previously used.
options.recommendation: Category 1 options: Cabozantinib, Regorafenib. Other Recommended: First-line regimens if not previously used (with precautions regarding prior checkpoint inhibitor use). Useful in Certain Circumstances: Ramucirumab (if AFP ≥400 ng/mL), Nivolumab (if no prior checkpoint inhibitor), Dostarlimab-gxly (for MSI-H/dMMR), Selpercatinib (for RET fusion), Entrectinib/Larotrectinib/Repotrectinib (for NTRK fusions) [HCC-I].

options.label: Radiation Therapy
options.preference: Useful for symptom control and/or prevention of complications from metastases (bone, brain) or extensive liver tumor burden.
options.recommendation: Palliative RT is appropriate for metastatic HCC lesions causing pain or other symptoms [HCC-6, HCC-H].

options.label: Best Supportive Care or Clinical Trial
options.recommendation: Best supportive/palliative care is a critical option. Clinical trial enrollment remains encouraged [HCC-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable; treatment intent is systemic disease control or palliation.

nodes.criteria: Histologic confirmation of mixed HCC-CCA on pathologic review.

options.label: Liver Resection
options.recommendation: Liver resection is considered the standard treatment for resectable mixed HCC-CCA [HCC-C].

options.label: Liver-directed Locoregional Therapies
options.preference: May be appropriate for limited unresectable hepatic disease.
options.recommendation: Though prospective data are lacking, liver-directed locoregional therapies may be appropriate for patients with a limited extent of unresectable hepatic disease, similar to management for HCC and iCCA [HCC-C].

options.label: First-Line Systemic Therapy for Advanced Disease
options.recommendation: For advanced stages, a regimen of cisplatin/gemcitabine chemotherapy combined with either durvalumab or pembrolizumab is an appropriate first-line choice, as these combinations include agents with anti-tumor activity in both CCA and HCC histologies. Tumor multigene panel testing (MGPT) should be considered to identify targetable alterations [HCC-C].

options.label: Subsequent-Line Systemic Therapy
options.preference: Depends on molecular profile and prior therapy.
options.recommendation: At progression, molecularly targeted therapies should be considered if the tumor harbors a targetable aberration. In the absence of a targetable aberration, regimens with demonstrated activity in both HCC and CCA are reasonable, including ipilimumab + nivolumab or regorafenib. A repeat biopsy at progression may be warranted to reassess dominant histology [HCC-C].

adjuvant_logic.summary: The guideline does not provide specific recommendations for adjuvant therapy after resection of mixed HCC-CCA.

pretreatment_evaluation.category: Clinical Presentation and Workup: HCC Confirmed

items.text: Multidisciplinary evaluation to assess liver reserve and comorbidity, and staging
Hepatitis panel (HBsAg, HBeAg, HBeAb, HBV DNA, HBcAb IgG, HCV Ab; if positive, check viral load)
items.recommendation: Recommended to determine etiology and manage viral hepatitis [HCC-3].

items.text: Bilirubin, transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, prothrombin time/INR, albumin, BUN, creatinine
items.recommendation: Essential for liver function assessment (CTP score) and renal function [HCC-3, HCC-E].
Required; platelets are a surrogate for portal hypertension [HCC-3, HCC-E].
Baseline serum tumor marker. Useful for diagnosis and monitoring [HCC-3, HCC-J].

items.text: Chest CT (with contrast if concurrently acquired with abdomen/pelvis scan; without if MRI is used)
items.recommendation: Recommended to evaluate for pulmonary metastases [HCC-3, HCC-A].

items.text: Abdomen/pelvis multiphasic CT or MRI with contrast
items.recommendation: Preferred for staging. Assess tumor number, size, vascular invasion, extrahepatic disease, and portal hypertension [HCC-3, HCC-A].

items.text: Bone scan if clinically indicated (e.g., bone pain, suspicious lesion on imaging)
items.recommendation: Selective use for evaluating potential bone metastases [HCC-3, HCC-A].
For comprehensive care of underlying liver disease, antiviral therapy, and management of complications [HCC-3].

items.text: Assessment of portal hypertension (varices, splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia)
Consider biopsy if imaging is not consistent or to confirm diagnosis if it does not meet AASLD or LI-RADS 5 criteria
items.recommendation: Core needle biopsy may be considered in specific scenarios as outlined in HCC-B [HCC-B].

items.text: Ultrasound (US) with alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) every 6 months for screening in at-risk populations (CTP A/B cirrhosis, CHB without cirrhosis)
Multiphasic abdominal contrast-enhanced CT or MRI for diagnosis and staging
items.recommendation: Preferred modalities to establish diagnosis, assess tumor burden, and for response assessment [HCC-A].

items.text: Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) as a problem-solving tool for indeterminate nodules at centers with expertise
items.recommendation: Not suitable for whole-liver assessment, surveillance, or staging [HCC-A].

items.text: FDG-PET/CT has limited sensitivity but high specificity; may be considered for equivocal findings
items.recommendation: Not routine; prognostic implications noted for increased metabolic activity [HCC-A].

surgery.role: Hepatic resection and liver transplantation are potentially curative treatment options for patients with early-stage HCC who meet specific criteria. They are the preferred treatments for patients who meet resection and/or transplant criteria [HCC-4, MS-41].
principles: Patients must be medically fit for a major operation and evaluated by an MDT [HCC-F].
All patients should be evaluated for possible transplant candidacy [HCC-F].

Resection requires adequate liver function (generally CTP A, no portal hypertension), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, and adequate future liver remnant (≥20% without cirrhosis, ≥30-40% with CTP A cirrhosis) [HCC-F].

Transplantation follows UNOS criteria (single tumor ≥2 cm ≤5 cm, or 2-3 tumors ≥1 cm ≤3 cm; AFP ≤1000 ng/mL; no macrovascular invasion; no extrahepatic disease) or expanded/downstaged criteria [HCC-4, HCC-F].
Minimally invasive approaches by experienced surgeons are safe and effective [MS-14].

procedures.name: Partial Hepatectomy
procedures.approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic by experienced surgeons. Involves removal of the tumor-bearing portion of the liver.
procedures.indications: Potentially resectable HCC in patients with CTP A liver function (or highly selected CTP B without portal hypertension), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, and adequate future liver remnant [HCC-F].
procedures.key_complications: Liver failure (especially in cirrhosis), bleeding, bile leak, infection. Postoperative clinical decompensation risk increases with preoperative portal hypertension [MS-14].
procedures.operative_mortality: Low (≤5%) in well-selected patients at experienced centers [MS-14].

procedures.name: Liver Transplantation
procedures.approach: Cadaveric or living donor orthotopic liver transplantation.
procedures.indications: Patients meeting UNOS criteria (as above) or downstaged to within criteria. Treats both the tumor and underlying cirrhosis. Preferred for patients with CTP B or C cirrhosis who are not surgical candidates [HCC-4, HCC-F].
procedures.key_complications: Surgical complications, risks of chronic immunosuppression, infection, renal dysfunction, malignancy. Pre-transplant mortality while waiting on list [MS-18].
procedures.operative_mortality: Variable; cited as a risk but specific rate not provided in source.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Hepatic resection is indicated for: adequate liver function (CTP Class A without portal hypertension; limited resection in selected CTP B patients), solitary mass without major vascular invasion, adequate future liver remnant (≥20% no cirrhosis, ≥30-40% CTP A cirrhosis), and suitable tumor location. Resection is controversial but can be considered for limited multifocal disease or major vascular invasion in highly selected cases [HCC-F]. Clinically significant portal hypertension (varices, splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia) is a relative contraindication, though its impact may be less if liver synthetic function is preserved [HCC-F, MS-14].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: For patients with chronic liver disease being considered for major resection, preoperative portal vein embolization (PVE) should be considered to induce hypertrophy of the future liver remnant and atrophy of the portion to be resected [HCC-F]. Other investigational methods to improve FLR growth include lobar Y-90 or PVE combined with hepatic vein embolization or arterial embolization [MS-15]. Evaluation of the future liver remnant volume and function is critical.

radiation_therapy.role: RT is a treatment option for patients with unresectable HCC, or for those who are medically inoperable due to comorbidity. It can be used for locoregional control or palliation [HCC-6, HCC-H]. SBRT can be considered as an alternative to ablation/embolization or when these therapies have failed or are contraindicated [HCC-H].
principles: Image-guided RT (IGRT) is strongly recommended when using RT, IMRT, and SBRT to improve accuracy and reduce toxicity [HCC-H].
Tumors may be amenable to RT (3D-CRT, IMRT, or SBRT) [HCC-H].
The majority of safety and efficacy data are from patients with CTP A liver disease, with growing experience in CTP B7; safety for CTP B8 or CTP C is not established [HCC-H].
Proton beam therapy may be appropriate in specific situations due to reduced dose to normal tissues [HCC-H].
Palliative RT is appropriate for symptom control from metastatic lesions (bone, brain) or extensive liver tumor burden in patients with CTP A or B cirrhosis [HCC-H].

dose_frameworks.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)
dose_frameworks.indication: Preferred for patients with 1 to 5 tumors, assuming sufficient uninvolved liver and liver tolerance. Considered as alternative to ablation/embolization or after failure/contraindication of those therapies [HCC-H].

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional Fractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: Historical or specific clinical scenarios not detailed further [HCC-H].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative Radiation Therapy
dose_frameworks.indication: For palliation of pain from extensive burden of cancer in the liver [HCC-H].

approaches.name: SBRT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Typically 3–5 fractions, total dose 27.5–60 Gy (BED10 >72 Gy preferred if constraints met) [HCC-H].
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not routinely specified for HCC SBRT in this guideline.
approaches.indication: Patients with unresectable HCC, not candidates for ablation/embolization or after failure/contraindication. Can be used for 1-5 tumors with sufficient liver reserve [HCC-H].
approaches.key_trial: Randomized evidence supports its usefulness (e.g., NRG/RTOG 1112 phase 3 trial) [HCC-H].
approaches.toxicities: Risk of radiation-induced liver disease (RILD), especially with compromised liver function. Requires strict adherence to dose constraints [HCC-H].

approaches.name: Proton Beam Therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: Hypofractionated; specific regimen not detailed but cited in references.
approaches.indication: May be appropriate in specific situations to reduce dose to adjacent normal tissues [HCC-H].
approaches.key_trial: Randomized clinical trials comparing to TACE or RFA cited [HCC-H].
approaches.toxicities: Associated with reduction of dose to adjacent normal tissues compared to photon therapy [HCC-H].

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: 8 Gy in 1 fraction for liver pain; other schedules for bone/brain metastases not detailed.
approaches.indication: Symptom control and/or prevention of complications from metastatic HCC lesions (bone, brain) or extensive liver tumor burden [HCC-H].
approaches.key_trial: CCTG HE1 trial cited for palliative RT vs. best supportive care [HCC-H].
approaches.toxicities: Depends on site treated; generally low for single-fraction liver palliation.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is indicated for patients with advanced/metastatic HCC (HCC-6) or for progression on/after locoregional therapy (HCC-5). Treatment selection is based on Child-Pugh class, performance status, prior therapy, and biomarker status. Most pivotal trials were in CTP A patients; use in CTP B requires caution and extrapolation [HCC-I, HCC-E].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: IMbrave150 trial showed improved OS and PFS vs. sorafenib [HCC-I, MS-33].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Durvalumab + Tremelimumab-actl (Category 1), Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Category 1, with counseling on early mortality risk) [HCC-I].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker required for these regimens. Endoscopic evaluation/management for varices required before atezolizumab + bevacizumab [HCC-I].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Other Recommended options: Durvalumab (Cat 1), Lenvatinib (Cat 1), Sorafenib (Cat 1), Tislelizumab-jsgr (Cat 1), Pembrolizumab (Cat 2B) [HCC-I].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Non-inferior or superior data to sorafenib in phase 3 trials [HCC-I].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Pembrolizumab is recommended regardless of MSI status; FDA-approved for MSI-H tumors in subsequent-line [HCC-I].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent-Line, after progression on prior systemic therapy
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CELESTIAL and RESORCE trials showed improved OS/PFS vs. placebo after sorafenib progression [HCC-I, MS-36].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: First-line regimens not previously used (e.g., atezolizumab + bevacizumab if no prior checkpoint inhibitor) [HCC-I].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ramucirumab (if AFP ≥400 ng/mL, Cat 1), Dostarlimab-gxly (for MSI-H/dMMR, Cat 2B), Selpercatinib (for RET fusion, Cat 2B), Entrectinib/Larotrectinib/Repotrectinib (for NTRK fusions) [HCC-I].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: AFP ≥400 ng/mL for ramucirumab. MSI-H/dMMR for dostarlimab. RET gene fusion for selpercatinib. NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion for entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib [HCC-I].

key_regimens.name: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, proteinuria, diarrhea, fatigue, immune-mediated adverse events. Requires pre-treatment endoscopy for varices [HCC-I, MS-33].

key_regimens.name: Durvalumab + Tremelimumab-actl (STRIDE regimen)

drugs.name: Durvalumab
drugs.day: Starting 30 days after tremelimumab, then every 4 weeks

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse events, diarrhea/colitis, hepatitis [HCC-I, MS-34].

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.day: Every 2 weeks (starting with first ipilimumab dose)

key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 for ipilimumab induction, then 14 for nivolumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse events; higher rate of early death (first 6 months) and high-dose steroid use (29%) per CheckMate-9DW trial. Counsel patients accordingly [HCC-I, MS-35].

key_regimens.name: Cabozantinib
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line, HCC after progression on sorafenib, CTP A
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, diarrhea, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, weight loss, fatigue [HCC-I, MS-36].

key_regimens.name: Regorafenib
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line, HCC after progression on sorafenib, CTP A
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue [HCC-I, MS-36].

key_regimens.name: Ramucirumab
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line, HCC with AFP ≥400 ng/mL after progression on sorafenib

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin + Gemcitabine + Durvalumab (for advanced cHCC-CCA)

drugs.name: Cisplatin
drugs.dose: N/A (refer to biliary tract cancer guidelines)

drugs.name: Gemcitabine
drugs.dose: N/A (refer to biliary tract cancer guidelines)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, immune-mediated adverse events. Specific doses refer to NCCN Biliary Tract Cancers guidelines [HCC-C].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment and Surveillance Post-Treatment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For locoregional therapy: Early imaging per local protocol can be considered. For systemic therapy: Periodic assessment with cross-sectional imaging of sites at risk (chest, multiphasic abdomen, pelvis). For all post-curative intent therapy: Surveillance imaging and AFP every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months, continuing for at least 5 years [HCC-4, HCC-5, MS-42].
response_logic: For locoregional therapies, response is typically assessed by the extent to which contrast uptake on dynamic CT/MRI is diminished (mRECIST criteria are more suitable than RECIST for HCC) [MS-21].

For systemic therapies, response is assessed by standard criteria (e.g., RECIST 1.1). On-treatment AFP changes can be a reliable predictor of tumor response, time to progression, and survival [HCC-I, MS-36].

After initial assessment, if disease progresses or is non-responding, the multidisciplinary team should reconsider all options: resection, transplant, alternative locoregional therapy, or subsequent-line systemic therapy [HCC-5, HCC-6].
imaging_recommendations: Multiphasic, high-quality, cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI) is the preferred method for surveillance and response assessment because of its reliability in assessing arterial vascularity [MS-42].
Multiphasic abdomen MRI or multiphase CT scans for liver assessment, CT chest, and CT/MRI pelvis [HCC-4, HCC-5].
Surveillance and AFP should continue for at least 5 years; thereafter screening is dependent on HCC risk factors [HCC-4, HCC-5].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If imaging is not consistent or to confirm diagnosis, consider core needle biopsy (HCC-B).

For patients with extrahepatic/metastatic disease, biopsy should be considered and is preferred to confirm histology before initiating systemic therapy, as noninvasive criteria are less validated in advanced stages [HCC-6, HCC-B].
In patients with advanced cHCC-CCA, a repeat biopsy at tumor progression may be warranted to reassess dominant histology, especially if there are discordant areas of response and progression [HCC-C].
If a growing mass has a negative biopsy, it does not rule out HCC; continued monitoring and multidisciplinary review are recommended [MS-9].

prognosis.overall: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is associated with a poor prognosis, with many patients diagnosed at an advanced stage. The 5-year survival rate for all stages combined remains low. However, prognosis varies significantly by stage at diagnosis and underlying liver function. The presence of cirrhosis, vascular invasion, and extrahepatic metastases are key adverse prognostic factors. Survival is heavily influenced by the stage at diagnosis, liver functional reserve, and performance status.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Resection: >50% (selected patients ~70%); Transplant (within Milan criteria): 85% at 4 years; Ablation (RFA for <3cm): >50% at 5 years.
by_stage.context: Includes very early (single ≤2cm) and early stage (single or ≤3 nodules each ≤3cm) with preserved liver function and good performance status. Curative-intent therapies are options.

by_stage.stage: Intermediate (BCLC B or AJCC Stage II)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: TACE: 1- and 3-year survival rates approximately 57% and 26% in one trial.
by_stage.context: Multinodular disease without macrovascular invasion or extrahepatic spread, with preserved liver function. Largely treated with arterially directed therapies.

by_stage.stage: Advanced (BCLC C or AJCC Stage III/IV)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Systemic therapy: Median OS ranges from ~11 to 23 months depending on regimen. Sorafenib: median OS 10.7 months. Atezolizumab+Bevacizumab: median OS 19.2 months.
by_stage.context: Includes portal vein invasion and/or extrahepatic spread, with preserved liver function but impaired performance status. Systemic therapy is mainstay.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Median OS generally <3 months with best supportive care.
by_stage.context: End-stage liver function and/or very poor performance status. Management focuses on palliation.

prognostic_factors: Tumor stage (size, number, vascular invasion, extrahepatic disease)
Liver function (Child-Pugh class, MELD score, portal hypertension)
Underlying etiology and severity of cirrhosis
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in HCC is unique because it depends on both cancer-related factors (tumor burden, aggressiveness) and liver-related factors (functional reserve, portal hypertension). Patients with well-compensated cirrhosis and early-stage HCC have the best outcomes with curative therapies. Advanced liver dysfunction significantly limits treatment options and worsens prognosis regardless of tumor stage.

disease_related.management: Manage portal hypertension (beta-blockers, endoscopic ligation, TIPS), diuretics, lactulose, protein restriction. Refer to hepatologist for transplant evaluation if appropriate.
May be considered for locoregional therapy (TACE, TARE) in highly selected patients with segmental/lobar invasion. Systemic therapy is primary treatment.
Emergency embolization or surgery; stabilize then treat underlying HCC.

immunotherapy: Immune-mediated adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic toxicity)
Higher incidence of early death in first 6 months with ipilimumab+nivolumab (29% required high-dose steroids)
targeted_therapy: Hypertension, hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea (sorafenib, lenvatinib, regorafenib, cabozantinib)
surgery: Post-hepatectomy liver failure (risk with inadequate future liver remnant)
radiation: Radiation-induced liver disease (especially with CTP B/C or inadequate dosing constraints)

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral throughout the management of HCC to manage symptoms, treatment toxicities, and underlying liver disease. A multidisciplinary approach including hepatology, palliative care, and nutrition is essential.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional assessment and intervention are critical due to frequent malnutrition and sarcopenia. Protein intake should not be restricted unless encephalopathy is present. Patients should be evaluated by a dietitian.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Follow ASCO/NCCN antiemetic guidelines based on emetogenic potential of regimen. Prophylaxis for highly emetogenic chemotherapy (e.g., cisplatin-based).
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Consider G-CSF prophylaxis for regimens with >20% febrile neutropenia risk. Monitor closely.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Risk assessment using Khorana score. HCC is a moderate to high risk cancer. Prophylaxis with LMWH or DOACs may be considered, balancing bleeding risk (especially with esophageal varices).
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain assessment using validated tools. Stepwise analgesia (WHO ladder). Avoid hepatotoxic drugs. Consider palliative radiation for painful bone metastases or liver burden.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Screen for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer. Provide resources for coping, financial toxicity, and advanced care planning.
supportive_care.dental_care: Evaluation prior to anti-angiogenic therapy to minimize osteonecrosis risk.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After curative-intent therapy (resection, transplantation, ablation), surveillance is recommended to detect recurrence or new primary tumors. Surveillance includes multiphasic cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI) and serum AFP every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months. Surveillance should continue for at least 5 years; thereafter, screening depends on ongoing HCC risk factors.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Earlier detection of recurrence may allow for repeat curative-intent therapy or eligibility for clinical trials. Recurrence patterns include intrahepatic recurrence (from metachronous new primary or intrahepatic metastasis) and extrahepatic metastases.
late_effects_screening: Monitoring for osteoporosis and fractures (related to cirrhosis, corticosteroid use)
Cardiovascular risk assessment (especially with anti-angiogenic therapy)
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence after resection is common (>70% at 5 years). Patterns include early recurrence (<2 years) often from intrahepatic metastasis (associated with microvascular invasion) and late recurrence (>2 years) often from de novo multicentric carcinogenesis in the cirrhotic liver.

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Every 3-6 months for 2 years after curative therapy, then every 6 months thereafter.
For patients on systemic therapy for advanced disease, assess response every 2-3 cycles (approximately every 6-9 weeks) using imaging and AFP.
Include history, physical exam, and assessment of liver function and performance status at each visit.
imaging_strategy: Multiphasic contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis is preferred for surveillance post-treatment.
CT chest to assess for lung metastases.
Use consistent modality and protocol for comparability.
Consider alternative modality if clinical suspicion discordant with imaging findings.
laboratory_monitoring: Serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) every 3-6 months post-curative therapy.
Liver function tests (bilirubin, AST, ALT, ALP, INR) regularly.
Complete blood count (monitor for portal hypertension-related cytopenias).
Assessment of viral hepatitis status and viral load (for those with HBV/HCV).
supportive_follow_up: Bone density screening if on long-term corticosteroids or with risk factors.

special_situations.key_message: Rare variant occurring in young patients without cirrhosis. Generally better prognosis but high recurrence rates after resection.
special_situations.management: Complete surgical resection is only curative option. No established systemic therapies; clinical trial enrollment encouraged.

special_situations.key_message: Accounts for 1-10% of primary liver tumors. Diagnosis often requires biopsy. Molecular profiling may reveal actionable alterations more common in CCA.
special_situations.management: Resection for resectable disease. For advanced disease, consider regimens active in both HCC and CCA (e.g., gemcitabine/cisplatin + durvalumab/pembrolizumab). Repeat biopsy at progression to assess dominant histology.
special_situations.evidence: References 1-5 in Principles of Mixed HCC-CCA.

special_situations.key_message: Systemic therapy options are limited due to increased toxicity and diminished efficacy. Most trial data in CTP A.
special_situations.management: Use with extreme caution. Consider atezolizumab+bevacizumab or nivolumab based on real-world data. Close monitoring for liver decompensation. Avoid in CTP C except in select cases or clinical trials.
special_situations.evidence: References 1-4 in Principles of Liver Functional Assessment.

special_situations.situation: HCC with Main Portal Vein Thrombosis
special_situations.key_message: Generally considered advanced disease. Largely treated with systemic therapy.
special_situations.management: Systemic therapy (e.g., atezolizumab+bevacizumab, lenvatinib, sorafenib). Y-90 radioembolization may be considered in selected patients with segmental/lobar thrombosis, not main portal vein.
special_situations.evidence: References 21-23 in Principles of Locoregional Therapy.

special_situations.key_message: Goal is to prevent tumor progression (dropout) while waiting. Bridge therapy recommended for eligible patients.
special_situations.management: Locoregional therapies (ablation, TACE, Y-90) as bridge to transplant. Downstaging for patients initially beyond Milan criteria with eligible tumor burden.

special_situations.key_message: Re-initiate staging workup. Consider repeat curative-intent therapy (re-resection, salvage transplant, ablation) if feasible. Otherwise, treat as per primary HCC stage.
special_situations.management: Multidisciplinary evaluation. Resection if localized and liver function permits. Systemic therapy for advanced recurrent disease.
special_situations.evidence: Pathway HCC-2 to HCC-6 in guidelines.

clinical_pearls: 1. HCC prognosis is determined by both tumor stage and underlying liver function (Child-Pugh class).
2. Multidisciplinary team evaluation is essential for optimal management at all stages.
3. Curative-intent therapies (resection, transplant, ablation) are limited to early-stage HCC with preserved liver function.
4. Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab and tremelimumab plus durvalumab are category 1 preferred first-line systemic therapies.
5. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is used for surveillance, prognosis, and monitoring response to therapy but is not diagnostic alone.
6. Transplant criteria (UNOS) are based on tumor size, number, and AFP level, not histology, due to high specificity of imaging in cirrhotic patients.
7. Bridge and downstaging therapies are critical for patients awaiting transplant to reduce dropout risk.
8. Systemic therapy in Child-Pugh B cirrhosis is not well-established; use with caution and extrapolate from CTP A data.

key_trials.full_name: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab versus sorafenib in unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Unresectable HCC, CTP A, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 19.2 vs. 13.4 months (HR, 0.66; descriptive P < .001). Median PFS: 6.9 vs. 4.3 months (HR, 0.65; descriptive P < .001). ORR: 27.3% vs. 11.9%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Duration of response >6 months: 87.6% vs. 59.1%. Grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events: 43% vs. 46%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established atezolizumab+bevacizumab as a preferred first-line systemic therapy option.

key_trials.full_name: Tremelimumab plus durvalumab in unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Unresectable HCC, CTP A, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 16.4 vs. 13.8 months (HR, 0.78; P = .0035). 5-year OS rate: 19.6% vs. 9.4%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant PFS difference. Serious treatment-related adverse events: 17.5% vs. 9.9%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established tremelimumab+durvalumab as a preferred first-line systemic therapy option.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus lenvatinib or sorafenib as first-line treatment for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Unresectable HCC, CTP A/B, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median DOR: 30.4 vs. 12.9 months. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events: 41% vs. 42%.
key_trials.practice_change: Moved ipilimumab+nivolumab to category 1 preferred first-line therapy. Noted higher risk of early death and immune-related adverse events requiring steroids.

key_trials.full_name: Lenvatinib versus sorafenib in first-line treatment of patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Unresectable HCC, CTP A, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 13.6 vs. 12.3 months (HR, 0.92; 95% CI, 0.79-1.06). Non-inferiority met.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR: 24.1% vs. 9.2%. Median PFS: 7.4 vs. 3.7 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Established lenvatinib as a first-line option comparable to sorafenib.

key_trials.population: Advanced HCC (not eligible for or progression after locoregional therapy), CTP A
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to radiologic progression: 5.5 vs. 2.8 months.
key_trials.practice_change: First systemic therapy to demonstrate survival benefit in advanced HCC.

key_trials.full_name: Cabozantinib in patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Advanced HCC, progressed on or after sorafenib, CTP A
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 10.2 vs. 8.0 months (HR, 0.76; P = .005). Median PFS: 5.2 vs. 1.9 months (HR, 0.44; P < .001).
key_trials.practice_change: Established cabozantinib as a subsequent-line therapy after sorafenib.

key_trials.full_name: Regorafenib for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma who progressed on sorafenib treatment
key_trials.population: Advanced HCC, progressed on sorafenib, CTP A
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 10.6 vs. 7.8 months (HR, 0.63; P < .001). Median PFS: 3.1 vs. 1.5 months (HR, 0.46; P < .001).
key_trials.practice_change: First FDA-approved therapy after sorafenib progression.

key_trials.full_name: Radiation segmentectomy for curative intent of unresectable very early to early stage hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Unresectable very early to early stage HCC, not amenable to RFA
key_trials.practice_change: Supports Y-90 radiation segmentectomy as a potential curative therapy for small HCC.

key_trials.full_name: Stereotactic body radiotherapy vs sorafenib alone in hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Locally advanced HCC, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 15.8 vs. 12.3 months (HR, 0.77; P = .06; HR adjusted for stratification factors: 0.72; P = .03).
key_trials.practice_change: Provides randomized evidence supporting SBRT in locally advanced HCC, though primary endpoint not strictly met.

key_trials.full_name: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab versus active surveillance in patients with resected or ablated high-risk hepatocellular carcinoma
key_trials.population: Resected or ablated high-risk HCC, CTP A
key_trials.key_result: RFS: HR, 0.72 (P = .012) at primary analysis. Updated analysis showed benefit not sustained.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events: 41% in treatment group.
key_trials.practice_change: Initial positive result led to exploration of adjuvant IO, but updated analysis tempered enthusiasm; not included as standard adjuvant option in NCCN.
